library.label: Library Access
library.notes: Library operating expenditure per capita from IMLS FY2022. Does not capture library quality, hours, collection size, or digital services. County totals aggregated from library-level data using Census tract FIPS mapping.

mobility.label: Economic Mobility
mobility.unit: mean household income rank at age 35 (percentile)
mobility.notes: Excluded from gravity model distance calculation due to temporal mismatch — reflects outcomes of children born 1978-1983 (measured through 2015). Using alongside 2022 current-conditions datasets would conflate different eras of American economic geography. Available for scatter plot exploration as a historical structural indicator.

air.label: Air Quality
air.notes: Inverted Median AQI: computed as 200 − Median AQI, so higher value = cleaner air. Annual county AQI summary from EPA monitoring stations. Only 973 counties (~31%) have direct measurements with 50+ monitoring days. Counties without monitors are absent (not modeled/interpolated). Monitor placement is non-random. Air quality is spatially smooth, making county-level aggregation appropriate (low MAUP).
air.direction_note: Inverted Median AQI: higher value = cleaner air (200 - Median AQI)

broadband.label: Broadband Subscription Rate
broadband.notes: Measures household broadband subscription rate from ACS (table B28002), NOT physical infrastructure availability. Low subscription rates may reflect unaffordability or lack of infrastructure — this data cannot distinguish between them. FCC availability maps would be needed to separate these causes. ACS 5-year estimate (2018–2022 average).

eitc.label: EITC Uptake
eitc.notes: EITC filing rate as share of total tax filers. ~20% of eligible households nationally do not claim EITC. Non-take-up is systematically higher in immigrant communities and areas with tax filing barriers. Low EITC rates may indicate: lower poverty, OR filing barriers, OR higher undocumented population — this data cannot distinguish between these causes. ZIP-to-county aggregation via Census ZCTA area-weighted crosswalk.
eitc.direction_note: Ambiguous: higher rate may indicate more poverty OR better program access.

poverty.label: Poverty Rate
poverty.source: Census SAIPE (Small Area Income and Poverty Estimates)
poverty.notes: Census Official Poverty Measure (OPM) from SAIPE with fixed national thresholds, NOT adjusted for geographic cost of living. $30K income means very different conditions in rural Mississippi vs urban California. The Supplemental Poverty Measure (SPM) adjusts for this but is only available at state level. Model-based estimates combining ACS, IRS, and SNAP administrative records.

median_income.label: Median Income
median_income.notes: Nominal dollar income without geographic cost-of-living adjustment. $50K in rural Alabama = substantially higher purchasing power than $50K in coastal suburbs. Model-based county estimates from Census SAIPE program.

bea_income.label: ACS Per Capita Income
bea_income.source: US Census Bureau, American Community Survey 5-Year
bea_income.notes: Per capita income from Census ACS 5-Year (table B19301). Nominal dollars without cost-of-living adjustment. Previously mislabeled as BEA data. ACS measures household survey income; BEA personal income (includes transfer payments) would differ.

food_access.label: Low Food Access
food_access.source: USDA Economic Research Service Food Access Research Atlas 2019
food_access.notes: Physical proximity to grocery stores. Defined as living >1 mile (urban) or >10 miles (rural) from a supermarket. Measures geographic access, not affordability. 2019 Atlas — pre-pandemic baseline. County values aggregated from census tract-level data using population weighting.
food_access.direction_note: Higher = more population lives far from a supermarket — worse food access

obesity.label: Obesity Rate
obesity.notes: Crude prevalence from CDC PLACES, based on self-reported height/weight (BRFSS). Self-reporting systematically underestimates BMI — people underreport weight and overreport height. Bias varies by region, education, gender. Model-based small area estimates partially smooth this but underlying survey bias propagates.

diabetes.label: Diabetes Rate
diabetes.notes: Crude prevalence of DIAGNOSED diabetes from CDC PLACES (BRFSS-based). Areas with limited healthcare access show lower measured rates even if true prevalence is identical or higher. Poverty correlations likely understate the true relationship due to diagnosis bias. Small-area estimates from BRFSS telephone survey.

mental_health.label: Mental Health
mental_health.unit: % of adults with frequent mental distress
mental_health.notes: Self-reported frequent mental distress (≥14 poor mental health days/month) from CDC PLACES (BRFSS). Systematically underreported in communities with higher mental health stigma. Geographic patterns may partly reflect stigma rather than actual prevalence. Diagnosis bias: areas with less mental healthcare access have lower reported rates.

hypertension.label: Hypertension Rate
hypertension.unit: % of adults with high blood pressure
hypertension.notes: Crude prevalence of DIAGNOSED high blood pressure from CDC PLACES (BRFSS-based). Areas with limited healthcare access show lower measured rates even if true prevalence is identical or higher. Poverty correlations likely understate the true relationship due to diagnosis bias. Small-area estimates from BRFSS telephone survey.

unemployment.label: Unemployment Rate
unemployment.notes: ACS 5-year estimate (2018-2022) of U-3 unemployment rate. Includes 2020 pandemic spike (national peak 14.7%). BLS Local Area Unemployment Statistics (single-year county data) blocked programmatic access; ACS 1-year only covers 848 large counties. This 5-year average is the most complete county-level source available. U-3 counts only active job seekers; does not include discouraged workers or underemployed.

rural_urban.label: Rural/Urban Classification
rural_urban.notes: USDA ordinal codes 1–9 where 1=large metro core, 9=completely rural (<2,500 pop, not adjacent to metro). Treated as continuous for correlation and distance calculations. This is a simplification — intervals between codes are not equal in real-world terms. Interpret correlations involving rural_urban as indicating direction (urban vs rural) rather than precise magnitude. Population density would be a truly continuous alternative. Based on 2020 Census population and OMB metro definitions.
rural_urban.direction_note: Ordinal 1-9. Higher code = more rural. Whether rural is 'better' depends on the outcome being studied.

housing_burden.label: Housing Burden
housing_burden.unit: % of renters paying 30%+ of income on rent
housing_burden.notes: Share of renters paying 30%+ of income on rent (HUD threshold). Excludes homeowners. Selection bias: when housing becomes unaffordable, price-sensitive households leave, so remaining renters can afford current prices. Housing_burden may appear LOWER in the most expensive markets because the poorest residents have been displaced. ACS 5-year estimate (2018–2022).

voter_turnout.label: Voter Turnout
voter_turnout.notes: Raw vote count (total votes cast) in 2020 presidential election, NOT a turnout rate. No denominator (eligible voter population unavailable at county level). Confounded by county population — large counties show higher values regardless of civic engagement. 2020 had highest national turnout since 1900 (66.9%), driven by pandemic mail-in voting. Patterns may not reflect structural engagement.
voter_turnout.direction_note: Raw vote count (not a rate). Strongly confounded by county population size.

broadband_avail.label: Internet Access Rate
broadband_avail.source: Census ACS 5-Year, Table B28002 (any internet subscription)
broadband_avail.notes: Excluded from gravity model distance calculation due to r=0.993 collinearity with broadband (ACS B28002_004E). Both are from the same ACS table B28002 — broadband_avail uses B28002_002E (any internet, including dial-up) while broadband uses B28002_004E (broadband specifically). Including both would double-weight internet access relative to all other dimensions. Available for scatter plot exploration. Original source: Census ACS 5-Year B28002.

pop_density.label: Population Density
pop_density.source: Census ACS 2022 population + Census Gazetteer land area
pop_density.direction_note: Higher = more urban. Neither high nor low is inherently better.
pop_density.notes: Continuous alternative to USDA Rural-Urban Continuum Codes. Highly right-skewed (Manhattan ~70K/sq mi vs rural Alaska <1/sq mi). Consider log scale for visualization. Computed as Census ACS population / Gazetteer land area in square miles.
